nativeRange.habitat: Permanent and semi-permanent shallow freshwater habitats: swamps, bayous, sloughs, ditches, canals, ponds, slow-moving rivers, wet meadows, and floodplain pools — typically with heavy aquatic vegetation, soft mud substrate for burrowing, and dense organic debris. Occurs in both clear and turbid (even polluted) waters.

decorPreferences: long footprint over depth (120 cm+) to accommodate eel-like body
soft sand or fine smooth-gravel substrate for burrowing
PVC pipe shelters or smooth caves sized to the animal's diameter
large driftwood pieces and rockwork to create hiding caves
dense floating plants (duckweed, water sprite, hornwort) to diffuse light and provide cover
subdued lighting — nocturnal species, bright light causes chronic stress
tightly sealed and weighted lid (will leave water during droughts; can climb or jump out of poorly secured tanks)
moderate filtration with no exposed intakes large enough to entangle the animal
moderate water depth (animal must be able to reach the surface to breathe air)

diet.notes: Opportunistic aquatic carnivore. In the wild eats aquatic insects, frogs, smaller salamanders, small fish, crayfish, snails, and occasionally small snakes and turtles. In captivity readily takes nightcrawlers/earthworms (a staple), crayfish, raw or thawed prawns and shrimp, lean fish chunks (smelt, silverside, tilapia fillet), and (with patience) large sinking carnivore pellets. Many specimens can be transitioned from live to thawed prey relatively easily. Feed adults to satiation once weekly; juveniles roughly twice weekly. Hand-feed only with long forceps or feeding tongs — bites are powerful, can break human skin, and are generally considered painful though rarely medically serious. Avoid feeder goldfish (fatty, thiaminase, disease risk).

avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed
shrimp, snails, and other invertebrates (will be eaten)
conspecifics (cannibalistic toward smaller individuals; aggression between adults)
other amphibians (will predate frogs, smaller salamanders)
live aquarium plants in the substrate (uprooted by burrowing)

summary: The largest salamander in the United States — a fully aquatic, eel-like amphibian reaching up to 116 cm with tiny vestigial limbs. Native to Southeast US swamps, it is a nocturnal ambush predator that delivers a painful bite and demands a long, escape-proof, species-only setup. Specialist-only.
careNotes:
The Two-Toed Amphiuma is the longest salamander species native to the United States, with confirmed wild specimens reaching 116 cm (45.7 in) total length and captive individuals documented living up to 27 years. The eel-like body has four vestigial limbs (the front pair bearing the diagnostic two toes), small functionally near-blind eyes, and a wedge-shaped head with powerful jaws. It is part of the small relict family Amphiumidae, which contains only three living species, all confined to the southeastern United States.

This is a strictly specialist species. Despite its trade availability (Aqua Imports stocks them), it is not appropriate for typical home aquarists. Adult size, escape behaviour, dietary demands, longevity, and bite risk combine to make it a long-term commitment requiring a dedicated species-only setup. Some US states list amphiumas as restricted wildlife — check local regulations before acquiring.

Tank requirements: A long (minimum 120 cm footprint), tightly lidded, fully aquatic enclosure of at least 500 L is generally advised for a single adult, with larger specimens benefiting from more space. Width and length matter more than depth; the animal must be able to reach the surface to gulp air. A soft sand or smooth fine-gravel substrate is needed for burrowing — sharp gravel can abrade the skin. Provide multiple PVC pipe sections or smooth-stone caves matched to the animal's body diameter as daytime shelters. Dense floating plants help diffuse light; this species is strongly nocturnal and stressed by bright illumination. The lid must be heavy, tight, and gap-free: amphiumas are known to leave water voluntarily during droughts, can climb damp surfaces, and have been documented escaping from poorly secured enclosures.

Water quality and parameters: Excellent water quality is essential. Although tolerant of warm Southeast US conditions in the wild, captives are generally advised to be kept on the cooler side (around 18–24°C); temperatures should not exceed roughly 26–27°C. No heater is typically required in temperate indoor rooms. pH is reported to be flexible across slightly acidic to slightly basic ranges; hardness is unspecified in most husbandry sources, but moderately soft to moderately hard freshwater appears to be tolerated. Ammonia and nitrite must be zero. Skin sensitivity to medications (especially copper-based and many fish treatments) is high — quarantine any new tankmates separately and avoid in-tank treatment.

Feeding: Earthworms (large nightcrawlers) are the staple of choice. Crayfish, raw shrimp, lean fish chunks, and large sinking carnivore pellets are also accepted by most individuals after a brief settling period. Feed adults to satiation once a week; overfeeding shortens lifespan and accelerates water-quality decline. Always offer prey with long forceps — bites are powerful and painful and the animal can latch on.

Behaviour and handling: Two-Toed Amphiumas are nocturnal, secretive ambush predators that spend daylight hours burrowed in substrate or coiled inside shelters. They communicate with conspecifics via faint underwater clicks. Handling should be minimised and done only with thick gloves and proper restraint — bites can break skin. Despite the dramatic 'congo eel' / 'lamp-eater' folk names, they are not venomous; injuries are mechanical only.

Setup checks: tightly-sealed lid (mandatory), moderate-to-strong filtration with guarded intakes, generous footprint (120 cm+ length), soft burrowing substrate, multiple body-diameter shelters, subdued lighting, no small tankmates of any kind.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Wild breeding occurs via internal fertilisation (spermatophore transfer) typically in winter through spring; females lay roughly 150–200 eggs in moist terrestrial cavities adjacent to standing water from June to July and coil around them for the approximately five-month incubation period. Captive breeding has been attempted but is uncommon; sources note that simulating seasonal temperature drops and photoperiod shifts is required, the enclosure must be very large with both an aquatic section and a moist terrestrial laying area, and conditioning involves increased feeding. Considered advanced/not reliably reproducible in standard home setups.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Used for general husbandry framing (tank size 125 gal / ~473 L, temperature 70–75°F / 21–24°C, tightly covered tank requirement, diet of feeder fish/crayfish/earthworms with possible transition to frozen foods, eel-like body description, lateral-line/scent-based prey detection).

additional.notes: Used for lifespan upper bound (27 years recorded in captivity), expanded diet list, breeding timing and clutch size confirmation, fossorial/sit-and-wait predator behaviour, and neoteny note.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Internal fertilisation via spermatophore transfer. Females lay ~150–200 eggs in moist terrestrial cavities adjacent to water and coil around them for the ~5-month incubation. Captive breeding has been attempted but is not routine; requires a very large enclosure with both aquatic and moist terrestrial laying zones plus seasonal temperature and photoperiod simulation.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Up to 116 cm fully-aquatic salamander needing a 120 cm+ escape-proof species-only tank (500 L+), powerful filtration, prey-only diet, and a 25+ year commitment. Delivers a painful bite, will leave water during droughts, and is legally restricted as wildlife in some US states. Not appropriate for typical home aquarists.
